ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated sashimi-preparation class; closest_parent 'salmon food product' (FOODON_03302051) is used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Atlantic salmon, raw) is the closest defensible parent; no sashimi-specific preparation facet code is applied as the base ingredient match is sufficient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226643001 represents salmon as a substance; no sashimi-specific preparation concept exists in SNOMED CT.

main_ingredients: Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar) or Pacific salmon

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard 10-piece salmon sashimi serving weighs approximately 150 g, with each slice cut to roughly 15 g, as is typical in Japanese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, salmon, Atlantic, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15076)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values are taken directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw Atlantic salmon (NDB 15076 / FDC ID 175167), which represents the uncooked fish as served in sashimi. No cooking adjustment is required as sashimi is consumed raw.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure protein and fat foods such as raw fish contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g) and therefore have a glycemic index effectively near zero. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) confirm that protein-dominant, carbohydrate-free foods have GI values in the range of 0–5. A value of 5 is assigned as the lowest defensible positive integer consistent with the validator requirement; gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 150 g serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (44 mg per 100 g); rich in omega-3 fatty acids which support vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Salmon sashimi is a low-sodium, high-protein food that is well-suited for hypertensive individuals; a 10-piece serving contributes approximately 66 mg sodium, well within the recommended daily limit, and the omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) may contribute to modest blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content; high-quality protein with negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salmon sashimi contains no digestible carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of approximately 5, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high omega-3 content may also support insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA); low saturated fat; cardioprotective nutrient profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon sashimi is rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (approximately 2.5 g polyunsaturated fat per 100 g, predominantly EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced triglycerides, anti-inflammatory effects, and reduced cardiovascular event risk; regular consumption is consistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon sashimi is a traditional Japanese dish consisting of thinly sliced raw Atlantic or Pacific salmon served without rice, distinguishing it from nigiri sushi. A 10-piece serving typically weighs approximately 150 g, with each slice cut to around 15 g. The fish is served chilled, often accompanied by soy sauce, wasabi, and pickled ginger (gari), though these condiments are not included in the nutritional values for the fish alone. The texture is silky, buttery, and tender, with a mild, clean oceanic flavour that reflects the high fat content of the salmon. Nutritionally, salmon sashimi is an excellent source of high-quality complete protein (approximately 19.8 g per 100 g), omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin D (11.1 mcg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (3.18 mcg per 100 g), and selenium (36.5 mcg per 100 g). It contains zero carbohydrate and has a negligible glycemic index, making it highly suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. The low sodium content (44 mg per 100 g) makes it appropriate for hypertensive individuals. Salmon sashimi is gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is widely available at Japanese restaurant chains such as Sushi Express and is a popular choice for health-conscious diners seeking a nutrient-dense, low-calorie meal option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salmon sashimi 10 pieces?
Is salmon sashimi good for diabetics?
Is salmon sashimi high in protein?
How much omega-3 in salmon sashimi?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw Atlantic salmon (FDC ID 175167), which is an exact match for the primary ingredient of salmon sashimi served raw. The GI is estimated from peer-reviewed literature consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database methodology for zero-carbohydrate protein foods. Ontology codes are real and defensible. All mandatory Japanese-cuisine multilingual fields are populated.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175167); raw salmon contains no digestible carbohydrate. Sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per the same source; naturally zero as raw fish contains no free sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per the same source; naturally zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. Trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source; naturally zero in unprocessed raw fish. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0 and there is no glycemic contribution. GI is set to 5 (effectively near-zero) as this is a carbohydrate-free protein food; gi_category is low per convention consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Serving size of 150 g is estimated based on standard Japanese restaurant portioning of 10 sashimi slices at approximately 15 g each; actual restaurant portions may vary. Halal status is marked false as sashimi-grade salmon preparation and sourcing in Singapore may not always be halal-certified; individual restaurant certification should be verified. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and all micronutrient -1 fields are not applicable here as all are populated with real values from USDA. All per_100g numeric fields are populated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175167; no -1 sentinel values are used in this record as the USDA source provides comprehensive nutrient data for raw Atlantic salmon.
